text:
This is the reason why I use Emacs as my main mail client: I can adapt it to my workflow.

But the mail client example also illustrates the difficulty of the problem. What would the user-extensible mail client look like? Some users would want a very simple extension system for adding the occasional button. Others would want more sophisticated programmability.  But how many users would be willing to learn a programming environments specific to a mail client?

The other approach is the Emacs Way: have an extensible platform and implement the mail client in it. Learning the environment is then more attractive, because it covers multiple use cases. But it's also more difficult. And then, if the idea ever catches on widely, we will see tens of such environments, all incompatible, and we are back to the extensible-app situation where learning the extensibility mechanism is hardly worth the effort.

text: I would assume a problem with v86 on the basis that Mu works fine with qemu, but so many other images work fine that I'm suspicious of a corner case in Mu's output. If I have some time tomorrow, I might do some more testing myself with a minimal bootable thing. v86 strikes me as a nice tool for pedagogy, probably in the context of an online essay about programming at this layer.

text:
Right, significant tooling would be required for any one element of making this kind of pair programming:
1. *on demand*, where you can have people you don’t know come and help you synchronously with no friction
2. *many-to-one*, where lots of people could be helping you without it being overwhelming (text-based, working with your flow instead of interrupting it)
3. *skill-level-flexible*, where you can’t trust that the edits your collaborators are making are good and have to have some sort of lightweight, semi-synchronous review process
If you had such tooling, though, then there seem to be many areas where this kind of assistance could be either fun (Twitch streaming, audience competing to get code snippets accepted by Jon Blow on stream) or affordable (companies/developers paying for narrow specialists to increase their velocity and code quality while they work).

text: While not exactly related to the future of coding, I found this insight into coding in biology very interesting to see what’s possible to encode with a few characters in different combinations: <https://berthub.eu/articles/posts/reverse-engineering-source-code-of-the-biontech-pfizer-vaccine/|https://berthub.eu/articles/posts/reverse-engineering-source-code-of-the-biontech-pfizer-vaccine/>
